channel.title: Life on Christos Tsanikidis - CS, Math, Life
channel.description: Recent content in Life on Christos Tsanikidis - CS, Math, Life

item.title: Modelling and Simulating a Pandemic
item.description: Disclaimer: This is a rough model. The author is not an epidemiologist. The model was built based on his anecdotal experience. Warning:This is intended for large enough screens. You can’t see the whole enviornment using your phone. Adjust the Parameters below to explore the spread of the disease under different scenarios. Bear in Mind that some parameters might lead to poor performance depending on your device. Population Density Town Size Connections Radius Connectivity Chance Infection Chance Mean Days Sick Can you get Reinfected?

item.title: Learning by Generalizing and Exploring
item.description: When learning, the following principles can be very useful: generalizing what we learn, exploring the concept we learn, naming things. Generalizing-Exploring helps us learn more than what we are actually taught while naming help us recognize-apply what we learn in random occurances in our lives [1]. When we learn a new practical technique, the steps required to start incorporating it in our lives are: understand the technique/advice, make it a habit (the hard part).

item.title: Steps taken for the creation of a virtual football betting game
item.description: -- I have already dedicated a previous post on the simulation of the doubling method. In this post I will walk you through steps that can be taken for the creation of a virtual betting game. In particular we will examine a football betting game, similar to one currently found in the market. Design decisions Initially, before we develop the simulation, we can discuss two different possible methods to create such a game.

item.title: A Gambler, enlightened - A simple simulation of the Doubling Method
item.description: When I’m having conversations with people about gambling, I come across massive misinformation and misunderstanding, and should I attempt to explain, the normal person, understandably, struggles to understand in a casual conversation that probabilities can guarantee your loss in long term with numerous fallacies [3] complicating that attempt. The problem is exacerbated when we consider games that intentionally convolute the determining mechanism, making it harder to infer that you are deemed (again in the long run) to lose your money.

item.title: Hierarchies - Logarithmic counting for humans
item.description: Assumption: Rearrangement of objects for counting is possible. People are capable of processing and understanding a very specific range of patterns with ease. Likely a subset of those relevant to our past survivability. For instance we are unable to enumerate instantly small number of objects - for today’s requirements - and therefore we have to reduce the problem into maintanable chunks, appropriate for our capabilities. A way to reduce the problem is building hierarchies.
